pages.title: Functions \(L\)L modulo \(\ell^n\)l n and modulo \(p\)p
pages.html:
3 Functions \(L\)L modulo \(\ell^n\)l n and modulo \(p\)p

In this presentation, I prove a formula similar to the theorem of the report on the formula of traces (this volume; quoted "Report" later) for a ring of coefficients \(A\) which is either of torsion first to \(p\), or a field of characteristic \(p\). An essential use will be made of [2].

2 Theorem

2.1

Let's take over the ratings of (Report, §1) For \(A\) a commutative torsion noetherian ring, \(X_0\) a separate pattern of the finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) and \(\mathscr{K}_0\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}(X_0;A)\), we place

\[ L(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \prod_{x\in |X_0|} \det\left(1-F_x^* t^{\deg(x)}, \mathscr{K}\right)^{-1} \]

(1.7, 1.13 and Report, 1.5, 1.6).

Theorem. In each of the following two cases:

  1. \(A\) is of first torsion to \(p\);

  2. \(A\) is reduced and characteristic \(p\)

We've got

\[ \tag{2.2.1} L(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \det\left(1-F^* t^f, \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\right)^{-1} \text{.} \]

Theorem.

2.3 Note

In case (a), the method of reporting, §3 allows to deduce from the formula of traces (Report 4.10) that the logarithmic derivatives of the two members of (2.2.1) are equal. The ring \(A\) being twisted, this is not enough to prove 2.2.

2.4. Note

If \(A\) is a field, we have

\[ L(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \prod_i L\left(X_0,\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^i(\mathscr{K}_0)\right)^{(-1)^i} \]

and the spectral sequence \(E_2^{p q} = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(X,\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^q(\mathscr{K}))\Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^{p+q}(X,\mathscr{K})\) shows that

\begin{align*} \det\left(1-F^* t, \mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\right) &= \prod_n \det\left(1-F^* t^f,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{K})\right)^{(-1)^n} \\ &= \prod_{p,q} \det\left(1-F^* t,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^p(X,\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^q(\mathscr{K}))\right)^{(-1)^{p+1}} \text{.} \end{align*}

This brings back 2.2 (for \(A\) a field) in the following particular case.

2.4.1

If \(\mathscr{G}_0\) is a constructible sheaf of \(A\)-vectoral spaces, we have

\[ L(X_0,\mathscr{G}_0) = \prod_n \det\left(1-F^* t^f,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathscr{G})\right)^{(-1)^{n+1}} \text{.} \]

2.5. Note

It is easy to reduce the case (b) to that where \(A\) is a field (even a finite field) of characteristic \(p\), cf. 4.2.

2.6. Note

The particular case of (b) where \(A=\mathbb{Z}/p\), and where \(X\) is reduced over constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/p\) is degree \(0\);

\[ Z(X_0,t) = \prod_{x\in |X_0|} \left(1-t^{\deg(x)}\right)^{-1} \equiv \prod_n \det\left(1-F^* t^f,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^n(X,\mathbb{Z}/p)\right)^{(-1)^{n+1}} \pmod p \]

had been proved by N. Katz [16]. Its method, quite different from that followed here, starts from the theory \(p\)-adic of \(Z(X_0,t)\).

2.7. Note

Contrary to what I had unwisely told friends, one cannot in (b) omit the hypothesis "\(A\) reduced." For a counter example, cf. 4.5.

2.8 Plan of proof of 2.2

A translation of the degrees 1.7.2 It is assumed that \(\mathscr{K}_0\) is a limited, to a positive degree, complex of \(A\)-constructible and flat modules. We then have \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{parf}^{\geqslant 0}(A)\) (Report, evidence) 4.9, b). We will use 1.8 to develop the two members of (2.2.1) in series of powers of \(T=t^f\). The equality of coefficients of \(T^n\) will deduct from [2] and a trace formula on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n(X)\); in case (a), the formula (Report, 4.10), and in case (b) a formula which will be proved in the next chapter. It is necessary to be reduced in advance to the case where the symmetrical powers of \(X_0\) exist (e.g. in case \(X\) is affine) using the multiplicity properties in \(X_0\) of the two members of (2.2.1): for \(U_0\) an open \(X_0\), additional closed \(Y_0\), we have \(L(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0)=L(U_0,\mathscr{K}_0)\cdot L(Y_0,\mathscr{K}_0)\) and the similar formula for the right-hand member results from 1.7.1 by the method of (Report §6A).

2.9 The case where \(X_0\) is over

The multiplicity in \(X_0\) of the two members of (2.2.1) returns this case to the one where \(X_0=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_{q^k})\). Returning to the definitions, we see that (2.2.1) is then equivalent to 3.4.

2.10 The first member

Based on 1.8, we have

\[ L(X_0,\mathscr{K}_0) = \prod_{x\in |X_0|} \sum_n \operatorname{tr}\left(F_x^*,\Gamma^n\mathscr{K}\right) t^{m\deg(x)} \text{.} \]

3 Theory of Artin-Scheier

3.1

In the following, a coherent sheaf on a diagram \(S\) will always be looked at as a sheaf on \(S_\text{and}\). Either \(S\) a scheme of characteristic \(p>0\). If \(G\) a locally constant sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)- finite-range vectors, \(\mathscr{G}=G\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/p}\mathscr{O}\) is a locally free coherent sheaf on \(S\). Note \(\Phi\) both endomorphism \(f\mapsto f^p\) by \(\mathscr{O}\) and its tensorial product with the identity of \(G\): \(\phi:\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\). Artin-Scheier's theory [2] asserts that the continuation

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathbb{Z}/p \ar[r] & \mathscr{O} \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \mathscr{O} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

is exact. By tensorization with \(G\), we find an exact suite

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & G \ar[r] & \mathscr{G} \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \mathscr{G} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

3.2

Suppose \(S\) is an open in a noetherian scheme \(\overline{S}\). Be \(j:S\hookrightarrow \overline{S}\) the inclusion morphism, and \(I\) a sheaf of ideals that defines the complementary closed \(\overline{S}\setminus S\). Note also \(\Phi\) the extension by functionality of \(\Phi\) to \(j_*\mathscr{G}\).

Lemma. There are consistent extensions \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}\subset j_*\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) such as

\[ \tag{3.3.1} \Phi(\overline{\mathscr{G}})\subset I\cdot \overline{\mathscr{G}}\text{.} \]

If \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}\) is such an extension, the following

\[ \tag{3.3.2} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & j_! G \ar[r] & \overline{\mathscr{G}} \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \overline{\mathscr{G}} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

ext exact. Lemma.

  1. Existence\(\mathscr{G}'\subset j_*\mathscr{G}\) a coherent extension from \(\mathscr{G}\) to \(\overline{S}\). For all \(n\), we have \(\Phi(I^n \mathscr{G}')\subset I^{p^n}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\). Either \(n\) large enough for \(I^{n(p-1)-1}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\subset \mathscr{G}'\), and posions \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}=I^n\mathscr{G}'\). We have \(\Phi(\overline{\mathscr{G}})\subset I^{p n}\Phi(\mathscr{G}') =I\cdot I^n\cdot I^{n(p-1)-1}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\subset I\cdot I^n\mathscr{G}' = I\overline{\mathscr{G}}\).

  2. Core \(\Phi-1\): On \(S\), the assertion results from 3.1It remains to be verified that a section of \(x\) of \(\ker(\Phi-1)\), on \(U\) spreads over \(\overline{S}\), cancels in a neighborhood of the reciprocal image of \(\overline{S}\setminus S\). Since \(x=\Phi(x)\), we have indeed by (3.3.1) \(x\in I\overline{\mathscr{G}}\); moreover, \(x\in I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}}\Rightarrow x=\Phi(x)\in\Phi(I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}})\subset I^{n p}\overline{\mathscr{G}}\), and \(x\) is in all \(I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}}\), therefore zero in the vicinity of \(\overline{S}\setminus S\).

  3. Overemphasis of \(\Phi-1\): We can assume \(\overline{S}\) affine, write \(\mathscr{G}\) as quotient of \(\mathscr{O}^n\); \(\Phi\) then rises into an application \(p\)-linear \(\widetilde{\Phi}:\mathscr{O}^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}^n\), and just check the over-ejectivity of \(\widetilde{\Phi}-1\):

    \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{O}^n \ar[r]^-{\widetilde{\Phi}-1} \ar[d] & \mathscr{O}^n \ar[d] \\ \mathscr{G} \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \mathscr{G} } \]

    The sheaf \(\mathscr{O}^n\) is the sheaf of the local sections of the diagram in groups \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\), and \(\widetilde{\Phi}-1\) is induced by homomorphism \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\) in \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\). This homomorphism is widespread. To verify that it is overjective, it is sufficient to see it after any basic change \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) (\(k\) algebraically closed) and on \(k\) algebraically closed, any spatial homomorphism between connected groups of the same dimension is overjective. Finally, a spreading and surjective morphism of \(\overline{S}\)-schemes induces an epimorphism between the corresponding sheaves on \({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\).

3.4 Frobenius and Frobenius

The absolute Frobenius morphism \(F_\text{abs}:S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the identity on the topological space underlying \(S\), and \(f\mapsto f^p\) on the structural sheaf. For \(U/S\) is a canonical isomorphism \(F_\text{abs}^* U=U\): the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{F_{\text{abs}}} \ar[d] & S \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r]^-{F_{\text{abs}}} & S } \]

is Cartesian. The ringed site endomorphism \(({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}},\mathscr{O})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}},\mathscr{O})\) defined by \(F_\text{abs}\) can therefore be described as follows:

\(F_\text{abs}^*\) is the identity on \({S}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\); homomorphism \(F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{O}=\mathscr{O}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}\) is \(\Phi\).

With this description, for any spreading sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(S\), the correspondence of Frobenius \(F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is the identity.

To give oneself a morphism \(p\)-linear of quasi-coherent sheaves \(u:\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{N}\) on \(S\) is to give oneself a linear morphism \(u':F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{N}\). In particular, with the notations of 3.5, homomorphism \(\Phi:\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) corresponds to \(\Phi':F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\).

Lemma. With the ratings of 3.4, the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ F_{\textnormal{abs}}^* G \ar[r] \ar[d] & G \ar[d] \\ F_{\textnormal{abs}}^*\mathscr{G} \ar[r]^-{\Phi'} & \mathscr{G} } \]

is commutative. Lemma.

All you have to do is check it locally, so you can assume \(G\) constant, in which case the lemma is obvious.

The same theory applies to the powers of \(F_\text{abs}\).

3.6

Let's now assume that \(\overline{S}\) or a diagram on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) (\(q=p^f\)) and in accordance with the reporting agreements §1, write \(S_0,\overline{S}_0,G_0,\mathscr{G}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\) instead of \(S,\dots\). Lemma 3.5, for \(F_\text{abs}^F:S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\), gives a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ F^* G_0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & G_0 \ar[d] \\ F^*\mathscr{G}_0 \ar[r]^-{(\Phi^f)'} \ar[r] & \mathscr{G}_0 }\qquad\text{then}\qquad \xymatrix{ F^*(j_! G_0) \ar[r] \ar[d] & j_!G_0 \ar[d] \\ F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 \ar[r]^-{(\Phi^f)'} & \mathscr{G}_0 }\text{,} \]

then, by extension of the scalar from \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) and transition to cohomology

\[ \xymatrix{ F^*(j_! G) \ar[r]^-{F_*} \ar[d] & j_!G \ar[d] \\ F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}} \ar[r] & \overline{\mathscr{G}} }\qquad\text{and}\qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},j_! G) \ar[r]^-{F^*} \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},j_!G) \ar[d] \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) }\text{,} \]

where the second line is obtained by extension of the scalar from \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) from the endomorphism \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-linear \(\Phi^f\) from \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S}_0,\overline{G}_0)\).

Artin-Scheier's theory provides a long exact continuation

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},j_! G) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

where \(\Phi\) is the extension \(p\)-linear to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})= \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\) of the endomorphism \(p\)-linear \(\Phi\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\).

3.7

If \(\overline{S}_0\) is clean on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), the \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})\) is finished in size and \(\Phi-1\) is overjective (cf. 3.6(b) Therefore,

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G) = \ker\left(\Phi-1 : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})\right) \text{,} \]

and \(F^*\) is induced by the extension \(\mathbb{F}\)-linear to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\) of endomorphism \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-linear \(\Phi^f\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S}_0m\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\).

Applying the lemma 3.8 below, we deduce the identity of

\[ \tag{3.7.1} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G)\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(\Phi^f,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\right) \text{.} \]

Lemma. Either \(\Phi\) an endomorphism \(p\)-linear of a vector space \(V\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\): \(\phi(\lambda x) = \lambda^p\phi(x)\). We pose \(F=\Phi^f\) (\(F\) is linear) and we note again \(\Phi\) and \(F\) the extensions respectively \(p\)-linear of \(\Phi\) and \(F\) to \(V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\). So \(F\) stabilize \(\ker(\Phi-1)\subset V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\) and

\[ \operatorname{tr}\left(F,\ker(\Phi-1)\right) = \operatorname{tr}(F,V) = \operatorname{tr}\left(F,V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\right) \text{.} \]

Lemma.

On \(V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\), we have \(F\Phi = \Phi F\): endomorphism \(p\)-linear \(F\Phi-\Phi F\) is canceled on \(V\), therefore everywhere. Write \(V=V'+V''\), with \(F\) invertible on \(V'\) and nipotent on \(V''\). The theory of endomorphisms \(p\)-linear assures that

\[ \ker(\Phi-1)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} V'\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F} \text{.} \]

Since \(\operatorname{tr}(F,V'') = 0\), we have

\begin{align*} \operatorname{tr}\left(F,\ker(\Phi-1)\right) &= \operatorname{tr}\left(F,\ker(\Phi-1)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(F,V'\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\right) \\ &= \operatorname{tr}\left(F,V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\right) = \operatorname{tr}(F,V) \text{.} \end{align*}

1. Existence\(\mathscr{G}'\subset j_*\mathscr{G}\) a coherent extension from \(\mathscr{G}\) to \(\overline{S}\). For all \(n\), we have \(\Phi(I^n \mathscr{G}')\subset I^{p^n}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\). Either \(n\) large enough for \(I^{n(p-1)-1}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\subset \mathscr{G}'\), and posions \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}=I^n\mathscr{G}'\). We have \(\Phi(\overline{\mathscr{G}})\subset I^{p n}\Phi(\mathscr{G}') =I\cdot I^n\cdot I^{n(p-1)-1}\Phi(\mathscr{G}')\subset I\cdot I^n\mathscr{G}' = I\overline{\mathscr{G}}\).

  2. Core \(\Phi-1\): On \(S\), the assertion results from 3.1It remains to be verified that a section of \(x\) of \(\ker(\Phi-1)\), on \(U\) spreads over \(\overline{S}\), cancels in a neighborhood of the reciprocal image of \(\overline{S}\setminus S\). Since \(x=\Phi(x)\), we have indeed by (3.3.1) \(x\in I\overline{\mathscr{G}}\); moreover, \(x\in I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}}\Rightarrow x=\Phi(x)\in\Phi(I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}})\subset I^{n p}\overline{\mathscr{G}}\), and \(x\) is in all \(I^n\overline{\mathscr{G}}\), therefore zero in the vicinity of \(\overline{S}\setminus S\).

  3. Overemphasis of \(\Phi-1\): We can assume \(\overline{S}\) affine, write \(\mathscr{G}\) as quotient of \(\mathscr{O}^n\); \(\Phi\) then rises into an application \(p\)-linear \(\widetilde{\Phi}:\mathscr{O}^n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}^n\), and just check the over-ejectivity of \(\widetilde{\Phi}-1\):

    \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{O}^n \ar[r]^-{\widetilde{\Phi}-1} \ar[d] & \mathscr{O}^n \ar[d] \\ \mathscr{G} \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \mathscr{G} } \]

    The sheaf \(\mathscr{O}^n\) is the sheaf of the local sections of the diagram in groups \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\), and \(\widetilde{\Phi}-1\) is induced by homomorphism \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\) in \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\). This homomorphism is widespread. To verify that it is overjective, it is sufficient to see it after any basic change \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) (\(k\) algebraically closed) and on \(k\) algebraically closed, any spatial homomorphism between connected groups of the same dimension is overjective. Finally, a spreading and surjective morphism of \(\overline{S}\)-schemes induces an epimorphism between the corresponding sheaves on \({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\).
3.4 Frobenius and Frobenius

The absolute Frobenius morphism \(F_\text{abs}:S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the identity on the topological space underlying \(S\), and \(f\mapsto f^p\) on the structural sheaf. For \(U/S\) is a canonical isomorphism \(F_\text{abs}^* U=U\): the diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ U \ar[r]^-{F_{\text{abs}}} \ar[d] & S \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r]^-{F_{\text{abs}}} & S } \]

is Cartesian. The ringed site endomorphism \(({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}},\mathscr{O})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}({\overline{S}}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}},\mathscr{O})\) defined by \(F_\text{abs}\) can therefore be described as follows:

\(F_\text{abs}^*\) is the identity on \({S}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\); homomorphism \(F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{O}=\mathscr{O}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}\) is \(\Phi\).

With this description, for any spreading sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(S\), the correspondence of Frobenius \(F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is the identity.

To give oneself a morphism \(p\)-linear of quasi-coherent sheaves \(u:\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{N}\) on \(S\) is to give oneself a linear morphism \(u':F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{M}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{N}\). In particular, with the notations of 3.5, homomorphism \(\Phi:\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) corresponds to \(\Phi':F_\text{abs}^*\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\).

\[ \xymatrix{ F_{\textnormal{abs}}^* G \ar[r] \ar[d] & G \ar[d] \\ F_{\textnormal{abs}}^*\mathscr{G} \ar[r]^-{\Phi'} & \mathscr{G} } \]
is commutative. Lemma.
All you have to do is check it locally, so you can assume \(G\) constant, in which case the lemma is obvious.
The same theory applies to the powers of \(F_\text{abs}\).

Let's now assume that \(\overline{S}\) or a diagram on \(\mathbb{F}_q\) (\(q=p^f\)) and in accordance with the reporting agreements §1, write \(S_0,\overline{S}_0,G_0,\mathscr{G}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\) instead of \(S,\dots\). Lemma 3.5, for \(F_\text{abs}^F:S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\), gives a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ F^* G_0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & G_0 \ar[d] \\ F^*\mathscr{G}_0 \ar[r]^-{(\Phi^f)'} \ar[r] & \mathscr{G}_0 }\qquad\text{then}\qquad \xymatrix{ F^*(j_! G_0) \ar[r] \ar[d] & j_!G_0 \ar[d] \\ F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 \ar[r]^-{(\Phi^f)'} & \mathscr{G}_0 }\text{,} \]
then, by extension of the scalar from \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) and transition to cohomology

\[ \xymatrix{ F^*(j_! G) \ar[r]^-{F_*} \ar[d] & j_!G \ar[d] \\ F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}} \ar[r] & \overline{\mathscr{G}} }\qquad\text{and}\qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},j_! G) \ar[r]^-{F^*} \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},j_!G) \ar[d] \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) }\text{,} \]

where the second line is obtained by extension of the scalar from \(\mathbb{F}_q\) to \(\mathbb{F}\) from the endomorphism \(\mathbb{F}_q\)-linear \(\Phi^f\) from \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S}_0,\overline{G}_0)\).
Artin-Scheier's theory provides a long exact continuation

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},j_! G) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r]^-{\Phi-1} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G) = \ker\left(\Phi-1 : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})\right) \text{,} \]

Applying the lemma 3.8 below, we deduce the identity of

\[ \tag{3.7.1} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G)\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(\Phi^f,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S}_0,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0)\right) \text{.} \]

We pose \(F=\Phi^f\) (\(F\) is linear) and we note again \(\Phi\) and \(F\) the extensions respectively \(p\)-linear of \(\Phi\) and \(F\) to \(V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\). \[ \operatorname{tr}\left(F,\ker(\Phi-1)\right) = \operatorname{tr}(F,V) = \operatorname{tr}\left(F,V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\right) \text{.} \]

On \(V\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F}\), we have \(F\Phi = \Phi F\): endomorphism \(p\)-linear \(F\Phi-\Phi F\) is canceled on \(V\), therefore everywhere. Write \(V=V'+V''\), with \(F\) invertible on \(V'\) and nipotent on \(V''\). The theory of endomorphisms \(p\)-linear assures that
\[ \ker(\Phi-1)\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} V'\otimes_{\mathbb{F}_q}\mathbb{F} \text{.} \]

pages.html:
4 Modulo trace formula \(p\)p

Theorem. Be \(X_0\) a separate scheme of finite type on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), \(A\) a reduced commutative noetherian ring and characteristic \(p\), and \(\mathscr{K}_0\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\textnormal{ctf}(X_0,A)\).

\[ \tag{4.1.1} \sum_{x\in X^F} \operatorname{tr}\left(F_x^*,\mathscr{K}_x\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(X,\mathscr{K})\right) \text{.} \]

Theorem.

4.2 Reduction in case \(A\)A is a finished field

A standard argument of passage to a limit brings us back to the case where \(A\) is finished on \(\mathbb{Z}/p\). For each maximum ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\), the image of each member of (4.1.1) in \(A/\mathfrak{m}\) is given by the same formula, with \(\mathscr{K}_0\) replaced by \(\mathscr{K}_0\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}_A A/\mathfrak{m}\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_\text{ctf}(X_0,A/\mathfrak{m})\) (for the second member, see Report 4.12) The \(A/\mathfrak{m}\) being finished, and the intersection of the maximal ideals of \(A\) being reduced to \(0\), the announced reduction is obtained.

4.3 Reduction in case \(A=\mathbb{Z}/p\)A=Z/p

Suppose that \(A\) or a finished field, and note \(T_A'(\mathscr{K}_0)\) and \(T_A''(\mathscr{K}_0)\) the two members of (4.1.1) If, by scalar restriction, we look at \(\mathscr{K}_0\) as a sheaf complex of \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)-modules, we have

\[ T_{\mathbb{Z}/p}'(\mathscr{K}_0) = \operatorname{tr}_{A/(\mathbb{Z}/p)} T_A'(\mathscr{K}_0) \]

and the same for \(T''\).4.1.1) is valid for \(A=\mathbb{Z}/p\), so we have

\[ \tag{4.3.1} \operatorname{tr}_{A/(\mathbb{Z}/p)} T_A'(\mathscr{K}_0) = \operatorname{tr}_{A/(\mathbb{Z}/p)} T_A''(\mathscr{K}_0) \text{.} \]

I say that, for everything \(\lambda\in A\), we even have

\[ \tag{4.3.2} \operatorname{tr}_{A/(\mathbb{Z}/p)}\left(\lambda\cdot T_A'(\mathscr{K}_0)\right) = \operatorname{tr}_{A/(\mathbb{Z}/p)}\left(\lambda\cdot T_A''(\mathscr{K}_0)\right) \text{,} \]

and therefore \(T_A'(\mathscr{K}_0) = T_A''(\mathscr{K}_0)\). It is clear for \(\lambda=0\). For \(\lambda\) invertible, let us note \(A(\lambda)\) the reciprocal image on \(X\) of the sheaf of \(A\)-free modules of rank one on \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{F}_q)\) for which \(F^*=\lambda\). The formula (4.3.2) is none other than (4.3.1) for \(A(\lambda)\otimes_A \mathscr{K}_0\).

Theorem. Been \(\overline{S}_0\) a projective and smooth curve, \(j:S_0\hookrightarrow \overline{S}_0\) a dense open \(\overline{S}_0\), and \(G_0\) a locally constant sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)-vectorial spaces of finished row on \(S_0\). We have

\[ \tag{4.4.1} \sum_{x\in S^F} \operatorname{tr}\left(F_x^*,G_x\right) = \sum_i (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G)\right) \text{.} \]

Theorem.

Proof▾

Apply 3.3 to \((S_0,\overline{S}_0,G_0)\). The coherent extension \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\) of \(\mathscr{G}_0\) of which 3.3 guarantees existence is locally free because it is free of twist (on \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\subset j_*\mathscr{G}_0\)) and that \(\overline{S}_0\) is regular size one. Endomorphism \(q\)-linear \(\Phi^f\) of \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\) can be interpreted as morphism

\[ u : F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 \text{.} \]

It is factored by \(I\overline{G}\), for \(I\) defining the closed \(\overline{S}\setminus S\). Either \(v:j_!G_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{\mathscr{G}}_0\) the only extension of the natural application \(G_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_0 = G_0\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/p}\mathscr{O}\).

\[ \tag{4.4.2} \xymatrix{ F^* j_! G_0 \ar[r]^-{F^*} \ar[d]^-{F^* v} & j_! G_0 \ar[d]^-v \\ F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 \ar[r]^-u & \overline{\mathscr{G}}_0 } \]

Finally, note \(u F^*\) the compound application

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r]^-{F^*} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},F^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}) \ar[r]^-u & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}}) , } \]

On the other hand (3.7.1)

\[ \tag{4.4.3} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(S,G)\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(u F^*,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})\right) \text{.} \]

For each closed point \(i_x:x\hookrightarrow \overline{S}\) of \(\overline{S}\), posions \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}_x = i_x^*\overline{\mathscr{G}}\). The formula of traces of Lefschetz in coherent cohomology ("Woodshole fixed point formula"), applied to \(F\) and \(u\), says that

\[ \tag{4.4.4} \sum_i (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left(u^* F^*, \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\overline{S},\overline{\mathscr{G}})\right) = \sum_{x\in \overline{S}^F} \frac{\operatorname{tr}(u_x,\mathscr{G}_x)}{\det(1-d F_x)} \text{.} \]

To the second member

  1. since \(d F=0\), the denominators are 1;

  2. for \(x\in \overline{S}^F\setminus S^F\), the endomorphism \(u_x\) of \(\overline{\mathscr{G}}_x\) is null, therefore also \(\operatorname{tr}(u_x,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_x)\);

  3. for \(x\in S^F\), (4.4.4) provides a contribution

    \[ \operatorname{tr}(u_x,\overline{\mathscr{G}}_x) = \operatorname{tr}(F_x^*,G_x) \text{.} \]

    Identity (4.4.1) is therefore the result of (4.4.3) and (4.4.4).

C.Q.F.D.

4.5 A Counter-Example

We show by an example that we can't, in 4.1, omit the assumption that \(A\) is reduced.

Either \(Y_0\) the elliptical curve on \(\mathbb{F}_2\) for which the eigenvalues of Frobenius are \(\pm \sqrt{-2}\). It has \(3\) rational points, and is supersingular. The involution \(\sigma:x\mapsto -x\) therefore has only one fixed point, the point \(0\). The quotient of \(Y_0\) by \(\sigma\) is a projective straight; that of \(Y_0\setminus 0\) by \(\sigma\) is therefore an affin straight \(\mathbb{F}_0^1\), and \(Y_0\setminus 0\) is a double unbranched coating of \(\mathbb{F}_0^1\):

\[ \pi:Y_0\setminus 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{A}_0^1 \qquad \text{, } \pi=\pi\sigma \text{.} \]

The sheaf \(\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\) is a free module of rank one on the algebra \(A\) of the two-element group \(\{1,\sigma\}\) on \(\mathbb{Z}/2\). This algebra is is isomorphic to that of the numbers on \(\mathbb{Z}/2\).

The sheaf of \(A\)-modules \(\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\) is our example:

  1. We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2)\). Since \(Y_0\) is supersingular, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\) for \(i>0\), and the exact long suite

    \[ \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\{0\},\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \]

    shows that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\). So we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\) and

    \[ \operatorname{tr}_A\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2)\right) = 0 \text{.} \]

  2. Since \(Y_0\setminus 0\) a two rational points, in one of the rational points of \(\mathbb{A}_1^0\), the substitution of Frobenius is identity, and in the other, it is \(\sigma\). So we've got

    \[ \sum_{x\in {\mathbb{A}^1}^F} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\right) = 1+\sigma \text{,} \]

    and \(1+\sigma\ne 0\).

The coating equation \(\pi\) is \(y^2-y=x^3\).

\[ \pi:Y_0\setminus 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{A}_0^1 \qquad \text{, } \pi=\pi\sigma \text{.} \]

The sheaf \(\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\) is a free module of rank one on the algebra \(A\) of the two-element group \(\{1,\sigma\}\) on \(\mathbb{Z}/2\). This algebra is is isomorphic to that of the numbers on \(\mathbb{Z}/2\).
The sheaf of \(A\)-modules \(\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\) is our example:

1. We have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2)\). Since \(Y_0\) is supersingular, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\) for \(i>0\), and the exact long suite

    \[ \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^i(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(Y,\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(\{0\},\mathbb{Z}/2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \]

    shows that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(Y\setminus 0,\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\). So we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2)=0\) and

    \[ \operatorname{tr}_A\left(F^*,\mathsf{R}\Gamma_c(\mathbb{A}^1,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2)\right) = 0 \text{.} \]

  2. Since \(Y_0\setminus 0\) a two rational points, in one of the rational points of \(\mathbb{A}_1^0\), the substitution of Frobenius is identity, and in the other, it is \(\sigma\). So we've got

    \[ \sum_{x\in {\mathbb{A}^1}^F} \operatorname{tr}\left(F^*,\pi_*\mathbb{Z}/2\right) = 1+\sigma \text{,} \]

    and \(1+\sigma\ne 0\).
The coating equation \(\pi\) is \(y^2-y=x^3\).
